ASTM International, formerly known as the American Society for Testing and Materials, has a long history that stretches back over a century. Since its founding in 1898, the organization has played a crucial role in developing standards that ensure safety, quality, and consistency across a wide range of industries. Here’s a closer look at how ASTM came to be, its evolution over time, and its ongoing impact on global safety standards.

## **The Origins of ASTM: Addressing Railway Failures**

ASTM was born out of necessity in the late 19th century, during a time when the rapid expansion of railroads in the United States was leading to frequent and dangerous failures in steel rail tracks. As the demand for rail transport grew, inconsistent and substandard materials were causing rails to break under the stress of heavy loads, leading to accidents and derailments.

In response to this growing problem, a group of scientists and engineers, led by Charles Dudley, a chemist from the Pennsylvania Railroad, came together to form ASTM. Their mission was to develop standardized methods for testing materials—starting with steel—so that industries could ensure consistency, safety, and reliability in manufacturing.

## **Early Focus on Materials Testing**

ASTM’s initial focus was on the rail industry and the testing of steel for railroad tracks, but its scope quickly expanded. The organization’s first published standard, ASTM A1, "Specification for Steel Rails," became the foundation for consistent material quality and helped reduce accidents and improve safety across the rail system.

As other industries recognized the benefits of standardized testing, ASTM broadened its work to include materials such as metals, cement, petroleum, rubber, and textiles. The organization's standards helped manufacturers create products that met specific, measurable criteria, improving both safety and efficiency in the industrial sector.

## **Expansion into Multiple Industries**

As the 20th century progressed, ASTM continued to grow and diversify its areas of standardization. By the mid-1900s, the organization was developing standards for industries such as construction, aerospace, chemicals, and consumer products. Its reach extended beyond materials to include processes, testing methods, and performance requirements.

The establishment of the ASTM Committee E11 on Statistics in 1947 allowed for the application of statistical methods in developing and refining standards, further enhancing the scientific rigor behind the organization’s work. This shift toward data-driven standardization helped ASTM gain a reputation as a leader in quality control and safety across many sectors.

## **Globalization and International Standards**

By the late 20th century, ASTM’s influence had expanded beyond the United States to become a global leader in standards development. In 2001, the organization officially changed its name from the American Society for Testing and Materials to ASTM International, reflecting its increasingly global reach.

ASTM International has continued to collaborate with organizations worldwide, including the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), ensuring that its standards are aligned with global practices. Today, ASTM has over 12,800 standards that are used in more than 140 countries, making it one of the most important standardization bodies in the world.

## **ASTM’s Role in Modern Industries**

Today, ASTM International’s standards cover a wide array of industries, including construction, manufacturing, energy, healthcare, and consumer products. ASTM’s work ensures the safety and reliability of everything from medical devices to environmental testing methods, and it plays a critical role in the development of new technologies and materials.

One of the organization’s most visible impacts is in the consumer product sector, particularly in products designed for children, such as bicycle trailers, toys, and playground equipment. ASTM’s rigorous standards help ensure that these products are safe for everyday use, providing peace of mind for consumers.

## **The ASTM Process: Collaborative and Transparent**

ASTM International is unique in that its standards are developed through a transparent, consensus-based process. Committees consisting of volunteers from industry, academia, government, and consumer groups work together to draft and refine standards. This collaborative approach ensures that standards reflect the needs and expertise of various stakeholders.

Once a draft standard is developed, it goes through a review process that includes public comment and revision. The final version is only approved if it gains the consensus of the committee, ensuring that all voices are heard in the process.

## **ASTM in the Future: Adapting to Emerging Technologies**

As industries continue to evolve, ASTM International remains at the forefront of developing standards for emerging technologies. Today, the organization is working on standards related to additive manufacturing (3D printing), autonomous vehicles, nanotechnology, and renewable energy.

ASTM is also heavily involved in sustainability initiatives, working to create standards that help industries reduce their environmental impact while maintaining safety and quality. As global challenges such as climate change and resource depletion become more pressing, ASTM’s role in ensuring sustainable and safe practices will become even more critical.

## **Conclusion: The Legacy of ASTM International**

From its humble beginnings addressing railway safety in the late 19th century, ASTM International has grown into one of the world’s most influential standardization bodies. By developing clear, reliable, and scientifically sound standards, ASTM has played a pivotal role in improving safety, quality, and consistency across a wide range of industries.

Whether it’s through the steel that forms our infrastructure, the medical devices that save lives, or the bicycle trailers that keep children safe, ASTM’s impact is felt every day. As industries continue to evolve and new challenges arise, ASTM International will remain a key player in shaping a safer and more efficient world.

## Contents

# ASTM International

ASTM International

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| ASTM's headquarters in [West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania](/wiki/West_Conshohocken,_Pennsylvania "West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania") | |
| Founded | 1902; 123 years ago (1902) |
| Headquarters | [West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania](/wiki/West_Conshohocken,_Pennsylvania "West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania"), U.S |
| Number of locations | Additional offices in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") |
| Area served | United States (1898–present) International (2001–present) |
| Members | 30,000 |
| Website | [www.astm.org](http://www.astm.org) |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/ASTM_International_logo.svg/250px-ASTM_International_logo.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Astm_hq_west_conshohocken_019.png/250px-Astm_hq_west_conshohocken_019.png)

**ASTM International**, formerly known as **American Society for Testing and Materials**, is a [standards organization](/wiki/Standards_organization "Standards organization") that develops and publishes voluntary consensus technical [international standards](/wiki/International_standard "International standard") for a wide range of materials, products, systems and services. Some 12,575 apply globally. The headquarters is in [West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania](/wiki/West_Conshohocken,_Pennsylvania "West Conshohocken, Pennsylvania"), about 5 mi (8.0 km) northwest of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). It was founded in 1902 as the American Section of the International Association for Testing Materials.

In addition to its traditional standards work, ASTM operates several global initiatives advancing [additive manufacturing](/wiki/Additive_manufacturing "Additive manufacturing"), advanced manufacturing, and emerging technologies, including the [Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence](/w/index.php?title=Additive_Manufacturing_Center_of_Excellence&action=edit&redlink=1 "Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence (page does not exist)") (AM CoE), the acquisition of [Wohlers Associates](https://wohlersassociates.com) for market intelligence and advisory services, and the [NIST](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology")-funded Standardization Center of Excellence (SCOE).

## History

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Logo_of_ASTM_International_%282002%29.svg/250px-Logo_of_ASTM_International_%282002%29.svg.png)

In 1898, a group of [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") and [engineers](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer"), led by chemist, industry leader, and proponent of standardization [Charles Dudley](/wiki/Charles_Benjamin_Dudley "Charles Benjamin Dudley"), formed the American Society for Testing Material (ASTM) to address the frequent [rail breaks](/wiki/Break_of_gauge "Break of gauge") affecting the fast-growing [railroad](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") industry. The group developed a standard for the steel used to fabricate rails.

In 1961 the name "American Society for Testing and Materials" was adopted.

In 2001, ASTM officially changed its name to "ASTM International" and added the tagline "Standards Worldwide".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2014, the tagline changed to "Helping our World Work better." ASTM International had offices in Belgium, Canada, China, Peru, Washington, D.C., and West Conshohocken, PA.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In April 2016, the [Safety Equipment Institute](/wiki/Safety_Equipment_Institute "Safety Equipment Institute") (SEI) became a subsidiary of ASTM International. SEI is an accredited third-party certification organization that certifies various types of PPE[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] to industry consensus standards.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On June 9, 2022, it was announced that the [European Committee for Standardization](/wiki/European_Committee_for_Standardization "European Committee for Standardization") (CEN) and ASTM International agreed to extend and expand a Technical Cooperation Agreement from 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Membership and organization

Membership in the organization is open to anyone interested in its activities.[[5]](#cite_note-membership-5) Standards are developed within committees, and new committees are formed as needed, upon request of interested members. Membership in most committees is [voluntary](/wiki/Volunteering "Volunteering") and is initiated by the member's request, not by appointment or invitation.

Members are classified as users, producers, consumers, and "general interest". The latter includes academics and consultants. Users include industry users, who may be producers in the context of other technical commodities, and end-users such as consumers. To meet the requirements of [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") laws, producers must constitute less than 50% of every committee or subcommittee, and votes are limited to one per producer company. Because of these restrictions, there can be a substantial waiting-list of producers seeking organizational memberships on the more popular committees. Members can participate without a formal vote and their input will be fully considered.

As of 2015, ASTM has more than 30,000 members, including over 1,150 organizational members, from more than 140 countries.[[6]](#cite_note-DetailedOverview-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The members serve on one or more of 140+ ASTM Technical Committees. ASTM International has several awards for contributions to standards authorship, including the [ASTM International Award of Merit](/wiki/ASTM_International_Award_of_Merit "ASTM International Award of Merit") (the organization's highest award)[[8]](#cite_note-ASTM_International_Award_of_Merit-8) ASTM International is classified by the United States [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") as a [501(c)(3)](/wiki/501(c)(3) "501(c)(3)") nonprofit organization.

## Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence (AM CoE)

In 2018, ASTM International launched the [Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence](https://amcoe.org) (AM CoE), a global initiative uniting industry, government, and academia to accelerate research, development, standardization, certification, and industrialization of [additive manufacturing](/wiki/Additive_manufacturing "Additive manufacturing") (AM). Headquartered in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), with a network of experts across the Americas, Europe, and Asia, the AM CoE focuses on bridging the gap between cutting-edge research and consensus-based standards.

The AM CoE’s main pillars include:

These initiatives aim to close standardization gaps, support qualification frameworks, and promote the broader adoption of additive manufacturing technologies across the [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"), [defense industry](/wiki/Defense_industry "Defense industry"), [medical devices](/wiki/Medical_devices "Medical devices"), [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry "Automotive industry"), and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") sectors.

## Acquisition of Wohlers Associates

In 2021, ASTM International acquired [Wohlers Associates](https://wohlersassociates.com), a globally recognized consulting firm and market intelligence provider in additive manufacturing and [3D printing](/wiki/3D_printing "3D printing"). Known for its authoritative Wohlers Report, Wohlers Associates delivers deep industry analysis, technology trends, and forecasts covering hardware, software, materials, and applications across the AM ecosystem.

Operating within ASTM’s Advanced Manufacturing Division, Wohlers Associates continues to provide:

## Standardization Center of Excellence (SCOE)

Funded by the [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology") (NIST), the Standardization Center of Excellence (SCOE) is a more recent initiative under ASTM International. SCOE focuses on advancing innovation and standardization for critical and emerging technologies, offering a coordinated framework connecting R&D, standards development, workforce readiness, and regulatory alignment.

The SCOE enables cross-industry collaboration to ensure that next-generation technologies are developed, validated, and deployed with trusted, globally harmonized standards.

## Standards compliance

ASTM International has no role in requiring or enforcing compliance with its standards. The standards may become mandatory when referenced by an external contract, corporation, regulation, law, or government.[[6]](#cite_note-DetailedOverview-6)

In the United States, ASTM standards have been adopted by incorporation or reference in many federal, state, and municipal government regulations. The 1995 [National Technology Transfer and Advancement Act](/wiki/National_Technology_Transfer_and_Advancement_Act "National Technology Transfer and Advancement Act") requires the Federal government to use privately developed consensus standards whenever possible. The Act reflects what had long been recommended as best practice within the Federal government. Other governments, including state and foreign, have also referenced ASTM standards.[[9]](#cite_note-TC-9)

Corporations doing international business may choose to reference an ASTM standard. All toys sold in the United States must meet the safety requirements of ASTM F963, Standard Consumer Safety Specification for Toy Safety, as part of the [Consumer Product Safety Improvement Act](/wiki/Consumer_Product_Safety_Improvement_Act "Consumer Product Safety Improvement Act") (CPSIA) of 2008. The law makes the ASTM F963 standard a mandatory requirement for toys while the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) studies the standard's effectiveness and issues final consumer guidelines for toy safety.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

URL: https://www.astm.org/news/press-releases/astm-international-additive-manufacturing-center-excellence-launches-consortium-materials-data-and

SUMMARY:
The mission of ASTM's CMDS is to bring key organizations together from across a broad range of industries representing the entire AM value stream.

FULL CONTENT:
![ASTM-newLogo](https://www.astm.org/v3/assets/blt5eb0a2cb04534832/blt51067b24c158463a/67bc579b2242644871b3c818/ASTM-newLogo.svg)
![ASTM-newLogo](https://www.astm.org/v3/assets/blt5eb0a2cb04534832/blt5f58c977f66e2026/67b2e4cde3c89c09fa55a3d0/mobile-astm-logo.svg)

###### 

###### 

ASTM International Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence Launches Consortium for Materials Data and Standardization (CMDS)

May 13, 2022

ASTM International Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence (AM CoE) and the founding industry members, are formally announcing the launch of the AM CoE Consortium for Materials Data and Standardization (CMDS) initiative. The mission of ASTM’s CMDS is to bring key organizations together from across a broad range of industries representing the entire AM value stream. The consortium members will collaborate on standardization of the requirements for AM materials data generation and will create and manage shared high pedigree “reference” datasets needed to accelerate qualification and greater adoption of AM technologies. The consortium is being launched with 21 founding members including:

“GE Additive is pleased to continue its engagement with the AM CoE Industry Consortium. As a company that offers the entire additive ecosystem, from feedstock to finished parts, we recognize the importance of developing highly pedigreed materials property datasets. By coming together to define a common approach to materials characterization, we hope to drive further understanding and expansion of the additive industry as a whole,” said Amber Andreaco, section manager – materials & powders, GE Additive.

The CMDS approach is particularly focused on determining key process-structure-property relationships necessary to develop methods for generating machine agnostic materials data. The CMDS will curate a shared database of high-pedigree data that will be used by consortium members to develop the tools, such as physics-based/probabilistic models, and data analytics needed to support rapid qualification of new AM applications, materials and technologies and real-time quality assurance to scale AM production.

“The Boeing team is excited to join the AM CoE Industry Consortium and collaborate with the AM CoE industry members to mature and accelerate the industrial adoption of additive manufacturing,” says Nicholas Mule, director, Boeing Additive Manufacturing Intelligence Center. “The collective AM expertise of the members provide the broad perspective needed to advance the AM industry and drive towards the common AM standards needed for sustained adoption.”

Each year, under the direction of the industry members and in coordination with key regulatory bodies and other government agencies, the CMDS will select materials and application specific properties (e.g., static, cyclic, thermal, corrosion) of interest and execute various projects that ultimately support the development of standards and datasets. While members retain exclusive use of the full datasets to support their business, research output and lessons learned will inform new AM standards and specifications developed through the ASTM related committees such as F42 to drive consistency across the industry, creating new and improved materials specifications with property and structure requirements based on robust datasets.

“This initiative connects industry, academia, and government teams to accelerate the path for additive adoption, moving additive manufacturing from a process with significant financial barriers to a standard manufacturing process for widespread use,” said Jeff Shubrooks, Raytheon Missiles and Defense Additive Manufacturing Technology Area Lead “Raytheon Technologies is bringing their experience and diverse research and data to help create the standards and necessary qualification methods.”

The AM CoE partners supporting the core technical activities and execution of the CMDS are ASTM International, NASA and Auburn University. This membership-driven consortium is intended to strengthen AM CoE’s core mission of research to standards (R2S) filling standardization gaps while enabling access to high quality datasets.To learn more, read the rest of the member’s perspective, and join the membership visit our website at: <https://amcoe.org/consortium>

**About the AM CoE**  
Formed in 2018, the Additive Manufacturing Center of Excellence (AM CoE) is a collaborative partnership among ASTM International, Auburn University, EWI, NAMIC, NASA, NIAR, and the MTC representing industry, government, and academia, that conduct strategic R&D to advance standards across all aspects of AM technologies. The center aims to accelerate the development and adoption of additive manufacturing by supporting standardization, developing training, certification programs, providing market intelligence, business strategy and advisory services via Wohlers Associates, powered by ASTM International. For more information, visit [www.amcoe.org](http://www.amcoe.org) and [www.wohlersassociates.com](http://www.wohlersassociates.com)

**About ASTM International**  
Committed to serving global societal needs, ASTM International positively impacts public health and safety, consumer confidence, and overall quality of life. We integrate consensus standards – developed with our international membership of volunteer technical experts – and innovative services to improve lives… Helping our world work better.

 Media Inquiries: Dan Bergels, tel +1.610.832.9602; [dbergels@astm.org](mailto:dbergels@astm.org)

CMDS Contact: Richard Huff, tel +1.202.991.6202 ; [rhuff@astm.org](mailto:rhuff@astm.org)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Founded in 1898, ASTM International aims to develop standards that ensure safety, quality, and performance across various industries, including emerging technologies like additive manufacturing.
Established in 2009, the F42 committee focuses on creating consensus-based standards for additive manufacturing to enhance quality, safety, and interoperability in the industry.
ASTM International boasts over 30,000 members from 140 countries, fostering collaboration among industry, government, and academia to address standardization needs in additive manufacturing.
ASTM has developed numerous standards for additive manufacturing, including material specifications and testing methods, to ensure reliability and consistency in 3D printing technologies.
ASTM emphasizes quality assurance and functional performance in its standards, facilitating the safe adoption of additive manufacturing technologies across various sectors and applications.
